html:
What to Do With a Tar-and-Gravel Roof That's Outliving Its Usefulness

A surprising number of Odessa's older commercial buildings still carry an original or twice-recovered built-up roof — layers of asphalt and felt under a bed of gravel, installed back when that was simply what a flat commercial roof was. Some of these roofs have decades of life left in them. Others are quietly failing under gravel that hides the problem until it's expensive.

Why Built-Up Roofs Have Lasted This Long

There's a reason BUR systems built decades ago on downtown Odessa offices and older strip retail are still standing. Multiple layers of felt and asphalt give the system redundancy that a single-ply membrane doesn't have — if one layer develops a weak point, the layers below often still hold water back. The gravel surface also protects the asphalt underneath from direct UV exposure, which matters given how much sun these roofs take over a service life measured in decades rather than years.

That redundancy is also what makes BUR forgiving of a maintenance program that isn't perfect. A lot of building owners in Odessa inherited these roofs with the property and never had a reason to think hard about them, because they simply kept working.

What the Gravel Is Actually Hiding

The tradeoff with a gravel-surfaced roof is that you can't see the membrane. Cracking, alligatoring, blistering, and worn spots are all happening under a layer of stone that most owners never disturb, which means problems tend to announce themselves as interior leaks rather than visible warning signs. By the time water shows up on a ceiling tile downtown, the felt layers directly above the leak have usually been compromised for a while, and the leak path along the deck can travel well past where the water enters the building.

We pull back gravel in test areas during inspection rather than relying on a surface walk, because a BUR roof that looks fine from a distance can be hiding blistered felt that's one hailstorm away from a real leak.

The Weight Question Nobody Asks

Gravel is heavy, and a lot of these roofs have been recovered more than once, meaning the original structural design is now carrying more dead load than it was built for. Before recommending another recover on an existing BUR roof, we check what the building can actually support, because adding another layer of asphalt and gravel on a structure that's already near its limit isn't a decision to make on assumption. This matters more on Odessa's older downtown stock than on newer construction, where structural margins were designed with fewer future re-covers in mind.

  • Number of existing roof layers and remaining structural load capacity
  • Blistering or alligatoring found under test-cut gravel sections
  • Drainage performance, since ponding accelerates BUR deterioration
  • Flashing condition at parapets and roof-mounted equipment
  • Whether a full tear-off makes more sense than another recover

Recover, Coat, or Tear Off

These are the three real options for an aging BUR roof, and the right answer depends entirely on what's underneath the gravel. If the felt layers are sound and dry, a reflective coating over cleaned, prepared gravel can extend service life at a fraction of replacement cost. If there's isolated damage but the overall system is structurally sound, a targeted recover with a cap sheet can work. If the felt is saturated or the structure is at its load limit, a full tear-off is the only option that doesn't just delay a bigger bill. We won't recommend the cheaper option if the roof's condition doesn't support it.

What Ignoring an Aging BUR Roof Costs

The owners who end up with the biggest bills are usually the ones who deferred a decision on a BUR roof for years past its useful service life, patching individual leaks as they appeared instead of addressing the system as a whole. Each patch adds a point of future failure, and eventually the roof reaches a state where a full replacement costs more than it would have a few years earlier, because water has reached insulation and deck across a wider area than the original leak suggested.

Questions About Built-Up Roofs in Odessa

How do I know if my BUR roof needs a full replacement?

We pull test sections of gravel to check the felt condition directly, rather than guessing from the surface. Blistering, saturated felt, or a roof already at its layer limit under code all point toward replacement over another recover.

Is a coating a real option for an old tar-and-gravel roof?

Yes, when the felt layers underneath are sound and dry. A coating won't fix damaged felt, but it can meaningfully extend the life of a roof that's still structurally healthy.

Can my building's structure handle another recover layer?

Depends on how many layers are already there and what the original design allowed for. We check this before recommending another recover rather than assuming the structure can take it.

Why does my roof leak in a different spot than where the ceiling stain shows up?

Water travels along the deck and insulation before it finds a path down through the ceiling, so the visible stain is rarely directly under the actual failure point in a layered BUR system.

Does gravel surfacing help with Odessa's hail?

Somewhat — the gravel absorbs some impact energy before it reaches the membrane, but a serious hail event can still displace gravel and expose or damage the asphalt layers underneath, which is worth inspecting after any major storm.
